content.text: Message passing produces a rich embedding for every node in a graph, but many tasks — drug toxicity prediction, molecular property estimation, graph classification — require a single vector representing the

. Graph pooling (also called readout) is the operation that compresses a variable-sized set of node embeddings into one fixed-dimensional vector, making graph-level inference possible. Choosing the right pooling strategy has a decisive impact on both expressiveness and training stability.

Think of graph pooling like a committee vote. Each node (committee member) holds a local opinion encoded as a vector. Global pooling takes the average, total, or extreme opinion across all members to form a group position. Hierarchical pooling goes further: members first form sub-committees that produce summary positions, then those summaries are merged again — like a corporate hierarchy reducing individual reports into a single executive summary. The deeper the hierarchy, the more abstract the final representation, but also the higher the risk of losing granular details.
Why Graph-Level Tasks Require a Dedicated Readout

Node-level tasks (semi-supervised node classification, link prediction) can use per-node embeddings directly. Graph-level tasks cannot: different graphs have different numbers of nodes, so stacking node embeddings into a fixed matrix and feeding it to a dense layer would require padding — wasteful, and it breaks permutation invariance. Any valid graph-level representation must be:
reordering the nodes should not change the graph representation.
the output dimension must be the same regardless of |V|.
it must preserve enough structural and feature information to discriminate between different graphs.
The tension between these three requirements drives the design of every pooling strategy.
Global pooling reads out a graph representation in one shot by applying a single permutation-invariant aggregation over all node embeddings produced at the final message-passing layer. Let
be the set of node embeddings after

Sum pooling is provably the most expressive of the three basic aggregators. The Weisfeiler–Leman graph isomorphism test (WL test) can be approximated arbitrarily closely with sum pooling but not with mean or max pooling alone — this is the key finding of the Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN) paper (Xu et al., 2019). Sum preserves the

of features: two graphs with the same feature distribution but different counts are distinguishable. The main drawback is sensitivity to graph size — a graph with twice as many nodes will tend to produce an embedding with roughly twice the magnitude, which can impede learning across differently-sized graphs.

Mean pooling normalizes by graph size, making it more stable across graphs of varying sizes. It captures the average feature distribution. The critical weakness: mean pooling cannot distinguish between a graph where one node has feature value 2 and all others have value 1, versus a graph where all nodes have feature value approximately 1.1 — the averages can be identical. More formally, mean pooling sees the underlying
of node features but ignores the
. Mean pooling is a sensible default for node classification transfer tasks where the proportion of node types matters more than their absolute count.
Max pooling extracts the most prominent feature value in each dimension across all nodes. It is the best choice when you need to detect whether

node in the graph has a certain property — a natural fit for molecular graphs where the presence of a specific functional group matters regardless of how many copies exist. Max pooling ignores how many nodes activate a particular feature and is insensitive to graph size by design. Its weakness: it discards fine-grained distribution information and can produce the same output for graphs that differ wildly in their node count.
Expressiveness: What Each Global Pooling Method Can and Cannot Distinguish
The three basic pooling operators differ in which graph properties they can detect. The table below shows what each one sees — and what it misses — given a set of node features.

A common practical remedy: concatenate sum, mean, and max poolings to get the benefits of all three. This triples the embedding dimension but costs nothing in expressiveness and often outperforms any single operator on empirical benchmarks.
Set2Set (Vinyals et al., 2016) replaces the fixed aggregation with a learned, order-invariant readout using an LSTM operating in an attention loop. At each of
steps, it computes an attention vector over all nodes, produces a context vector, and updates a hidden state:

for t in 1..T:
    q_t = LSTM(q_{t-1}, h_G^{t-1})            # query vector
    e_{tv} = q_t · h_v / sqrt(d)              # attention logit for node v
    a_{tv} = softmax(e_{tv})                  # attention weight
    h_G^t  = sum_v a_{tv} * h_v              # context vector
h_G = concat(h_G^T, q_T)                    # final graph embedding
Set2Set produces richer graph embeddings than simple aggregation but at cost: it runs
per graph. It is commonly used in molecular property prediction where graph size is small (10–100 atoms) and the extra expressiveness is worth the cost.

Hierarchical pooling progressively coarsens the graph across multiple layers, learning cluster assignments at each level. This mirrors how CNNs downsample spatial feature maps: early layers preserve local detail, later layers capture global structure. Hierarchical methods are computationally heavier than global pooling but can learn multi-scale graph representations — critical for tasks where both local motifs (e.g., rings in a molecule) and global topology (e.g., bridging structure) matter.
DiffPool (Ying et al., 2018) learns a soft cluster assignment matrix at each pooling layer using a dedicated GNN. Given a graph at layer
, two separate GNNs run in parallel:
clusters. The coarsened graph is then constructed as:

Two auxiliary losses are added during training to encourage meaningful cluster assignments: an entropy regularization loss (so each node is assigned confidently to one cluster, not spread evenly) and a link prediction loss (adjacent nodes should be in the same cluster). Without these, the model degenerates to random assignments.

from torch_geometric.nn import DenseGCNConv, dense_diff_pool
import torch.nn.functional as F

class DiffPoolBlock(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden, num_clusters):
        super().__init__()
        # Embedding GNN
        self.conv_embed = DenseGCNConv(in_channels, hidden)
        # Assignment GNN (predicts soft cluster membership)
        self.conv_pool  = DenseGCNConv(in_channels, num_clusters)

    def forward(self, x, adj, mask=None):
        # x: [B, N, F], adj: [B, N, N] (dense)
        z = F.relu(self.conv_embed(x, adj, mask))  # node embeddings
        s = self.conv_pool(x, adj, mask)            # assignment logits
        # dense_diff_pool returns coarsened (x, adj) + auxiliary losses
        return dense_diff_pool(x=z, adj=adj, s=s, mask=mask)

memory requirement for the dense adjacency at each layer, making it infeasible for graphs with more than a few thousand nodes. It is most commonly applied to molecular graphs (10–100 nodes) and small social network datasets.

MinCut Pooling (Bianchi et al., 2020) derives cluster assignments by minimizing the normalized MinCut objective — a classic graph partitioning criterion — jointly with the prediction loss. The pooling assignment matrix
is the output of an MLP applied to node embeddings, with the loss:

attrs.latex: \mathcal{L}_{\text{MC}} = -\frac{\text{tr}(S^\top A S)}{\text{tr}(S^\top D S)} + \left\|\frac{S^\top S}{\|S^\top S\|_F} - \frac{I_k}{\sqrt{k}}\right\|_F

content.text: The first term minimizes the cut between clusters (few edges cross cluster boundaries). The second orthogonality term prevents trivial solutions where all nodes collapse to a single cluster. MinCut Pooling is more stable than DiffPool in practice and produces cleaner, more interpretable assignments.
most informative nodes using a learned projection and discards the rest. For a projection vector
, a scalar score is computed per node:
The surviving nodes are gated by their score to ensure the gradient flows through the selection:
TopK pooling keeps the graph sparse (only

nodes and their induced subgraph), making it scalable to large graphs where DiffPool would run out of memory. The trade-off is that discarded nodes and their structural information are permanently lost.

from torch_geometric.nn import TopKPooling, GCNConv
from torch_geometric.nn import global_mean_pool

class TopKPoolNet(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden, num_classes, ratio=0.5):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden)
        self.pool1 = TopKPooling(hidden, ratio=ratio)  # keep top 50% nodes
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden, hidden)
        self.lin   = torch.nn.Linear(hidden, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        x, edge_index, _, batch, _, _ = self.pool1(x, edge_index, batch=batch)
        x = F.relu(self.conv2(x, edge_index))
        x = global_mean_pool(x, batch)   # final flat readout
        return self.lin(x)

attrs.alt: Graph pooling architecture: global pooling (top) aggregates all node embeddings in one step; hierarchical pooling (bottom) progressively coarsens the graph across multiple layers before producing the final graph vector.

content.text: In global pooling (top), all node embeddings
produced by the message-passing layers are aggregated in a single step into a fixed-size graph vector

, which is then passed to an MLP for prediction. In hierarchical pooling (bottom), the graph is progressively coarsened across pooling layers — large detailed graph → mid-size abstract graph → single super-node — before the final readout and prediction.
for hierarchical approaches. The following implements a full graph classification pipeline using concatenated global pooling — often the strongest flat-pooling baseline:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv, global_mean_pool, global_add_pool, global_max_pool
from torch_geometric.data import DataLoader

class GraphClassifier(torch.nn.Module):
    """GNN with multi-aggregation global readout for graph classification."""

    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden, num_classes):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden)
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden, hidden)
        self.conv3 = GCNConv(hidden, hidden)
        # 3 aggregations × hidden dim → final MLP input
        self.lin1  = torch.nn.Linear(3 * hidden, hidden)
        self.lin2  = torch.nn.Linear(hidden, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        x = F.relu(self.conv2(x, edge_index))
        x = F.relu(self.conv3(x, edge_index))

        # Concatenate three global aggregations
        h = torch.cat([
            global_mean_pool(x, batch),   # shape [B, hidden]
            global_add_pool(x, batch),    # shape [B, hidden]
            global_max_pool(x, batch),    # shape [B, hidden]
        ], dim=-1)                        # shape [B, 3*hidden]

        h = F.relu(self.lin1(h))
        h = F.dropout(h, p=0.5, training=self.training)
        return self.lin2(h)               # shape [B, num_classes]
) assigns each node to its graph index.

segments this tensor and applies mean reduction per segment, producing one vector per graph regardless of graph size. This is how PyG handles variable-size graphs in a mini-batch efficiently — all graphs are concatenated node-wise, and the
When to Use Each Pooling Strategy
Baseline; molecular tasks; when speed matters
Small molecular graphs needing high expressiveness
Large sparse graphs; sparse hierarchical encoding

The dominant industrial application of graph pooling is computational chemistry. In drug discovery, molecular graphs are classified by biological activity (active/inactive against a target protein). Datasets like MOLECULENET (Wu et al., 2018) benchmark pooling strategies on tasks like HIV inhibition, toxicity classification, and solubility regression — all problems where the

has a label, not individual atoms. A typical production pipeline: 4 GCN/GIN layers for message passing, followed by concatenated global sum + mean pooling, then a 2-layer MLP. DiffPool and Set2Set are used on smaller molecular datasets where the extra compute is feasible.

In computer vision, 3D point cloud classification (ModelNet40) is treated as graph classification after constructing a k-nearest-neighbor graph from the points. TopK pooling is preferred here because point clouds can have tens of thousands of points.
Using mean pooling when count matters.
Mean cannot distinguish a graph with 10 active nodes from one with 1000 active nodes if their per-node averages match. Use sum (or sum + mean) when the quantity of a feature matters.
Without the entropy and link prediction losses, the soft assignment matrix collapses to uninformative uniform distributions. Both auxiliary losses are mandatory, not optional.
Placing pooling before message passing completes.

Pooling after very few message-passing layers means node embeddings are still local and have not aggregated enough neighborhood information. Rule of thumb: pool after at least 2–3 message-passing layers.
Ignoring the batch tensor in PyG.
Forgetting to pass the batch index to global pooling functions collapses all graphs in the batch into a single graph embedding — a silent bug that trains fine but produces garbage predictions.
DiffPool requires a dense adjacency matrix at each layer — O(n²) memory. On a graph with 10 000 nodes this is 100M entries per layer, which is infeasible on standard hardware.
Concatenating sum, mean, and max is a strong, cheap baseline that outperforms any single aggregator on most benchmarks. Add complexity only if this baseline plateaus.
Choose sum over mean for count-sensitive tasks.
If the task involves detecting the number of structural motifs (rings, chains, functional groups), sum pooling is more expressive than mean.
Apply batch normalization between pooling layers.
Hierarchical pooling can produce unstable activation scales as the graph coarsens. BatchNorm between pooling and message-passing blocks stabilizes training.
Use residual connections with TopK pooling.
Adding a skip connection that passes the pre-pooling global readout alongside the post-pooling representation preserves information about discarded nodes and typically improves accuracy.
Tune the pooling ratio as a hyperparameter.

For TopK pooling, the fraction of nodes retained (e.g., ratio=0.5) has a strong effect on accuracy. Too aggressive (ratio=0.1) loses structure; too conservative (ratio=0.9) gains little from pooling. Search over {0.25, 0.5, 0.75}.
Graph pooling converts variable-sized sets of node embeddings into a fixed-size graph vector required for graph-level prediction tasks.

Sum pooling is theoretically most expressive (WL-equivalent with a universal MLP); mean is size-invariant; max detects feature presence. Concatenating all three is the practical optimum for flat readout.
Set2Set uses attention-gated LSTM steps for a richer but sequential readout — best on small graphs where the extra expressiveness justifies the O(T·|V|) cost.
Hierarchical methods (DiffPool, MinCut, TopK) progressively coarsen the graph, capturing multi-scale structure at the cost of additional parameters and compute.
DiffPool and MinCut Pool require O(n²) dense adjacency matrices and are limited to small graphs; TopK/SAGPool remain sparse and scale to large graphs.
In PyG, the batch index tensor is the mechanism that enables multiple variable-size graphs to be pooled independently within a single mini-batch — always pass it to global pooling functions.
